A Is 65?! I Finally Get What ASCII and Bits Actually Are

Back then I was on a "no more internet cafés" punishment from my dad. I'd still sneak off sometimes, but I'd keep it short to avoid getting caught. So I spent more time at the basketball court instead — basketball temporarily replacing video games as the thrill of choice.

Most days school ended around 4-5pm, then I'd play ball until 6 or 6:30, then go home. After a while though, I started swapping that ball time for sneaking into internet cafés. Anyone asked "where were you?" — I'd just go "playing ball" and leave it at that.
One time at the café, while I was playing CS, I saw a senior using Yahoo Messenger to flirt with his girlfriend.
Out of curiosity, our group of troublemakers waited for him to go to the bathroom, then sneakily opened his Messenger window. No sweet talk though — instead we saw a string of mysterious numbers:
language-txt shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
"Huh? Isn't that binary?" was my first reaction. We'd just learned binary conversion in class — I rarely retained anything from school, but somehow this one stuck.
When the senior came back, we teased him about flirting and then went back to playing CS. But those numbers stuck in my head. So when I killed him in-game, I casually asked:
"Hey senior, what did that string of numbers you sent your girlfriend actually mean?"
The moment the words came out I froze. His look turned into "kid, you're dead" mode, and I went stiff.
"It means 520, you idiot!" he shot back. (520 = "I love you" in Chinese number slang.)
You can guess how that went. He was pissed — so the rest of the CS round, he hunted me specifically, with headshots every time. My game experience went straight into the negative.
After he'd killed me god knows how many times, he finally cooled down. He smirked and said:
"Just check the ASCII table. You'll get it."

He didn't look at me — just kept playing and said:
"After I kill you a few more times, I'll tell you."
I shut up and took it. It wasn't until later in school, when we got to the topic of ASCII, that it finally clicked.
ASCII has a ridiculously long full name: "
nterchange". Strictly speaking the correct modern name is
US-ASCII only defines characters 0–127 (128 characters total).
So what is ASCII? In short, ASCII is a
— it defines which letters, numbers, and symbols map to which binary numbers.
The thing is, computers can't directly understand the letters, numbers, and symbols we use every day, so we need a standard that lets the computer "read" and process them.
ASCII is actually kind of like
from the movies — except ASCII uses binary instead of dots and dashes:
Whereas Morse uses dots and dashes:
So you can think of ASCII as "programmer's Morse code". But we're not here for Morse — let's keep the focus on ASCII.
Here's an ASCII chart for reference:

The whole conversion process has a clear pipeline — this diagram makes it more intuitive:
In computers, every character maps to a number and is stored as binary. ASCII defines that mapping. When you press A, the computer actually receives
"Then why do we even need decimal and hex columns?" you might ask.

We humans use decimal day-to-day, while hex is common in computer science and programming. So the ASCII table shows all three to fit the different audiences. On the frontend side, for example, CSS colours often use hex — like
language-css shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
So you can express colour as decimal or hex, but inside the computer it all gets converted to binary anyway.
"The final visual is determined by the font — typeface, size, weight, all of that."
So when A looks different in different apps, that's the font doing it.
How do we actually verify that A maps to 65? Easy. Since ASCII is the standard the computer uses, we can verify it with code.
Below is a small JavaScript snippet — paste it into your browser's Console and check the result.
"Wait, what's a Console?" you might ask.
The Console is a debugging tool browsers provide for developers. You can open it via:
After opening DevTools, switch to the Console tab — that's where code output shows up.
In the Console, you can verify ASCII mappings with JavaScript. Paste the code below, press Enter, and watch the output:

// Note: charCodeAt returns the Unicode code point, but in the ASCII range (0-127) it matches the ASCII code
Unicode is covered in the next post. Quick intro: Unicode is a much bigger character encoding standard that includes all of ASCII plus a huge expansion to support more languages and symbols.

You might've noticed: when describing binary, I keep splitting the digits into groups of four — like
Before explaining why, we need to revisit one idea:
Earlier chapters covered that computers store data in units of
The smallest unit is 1 bit — a 0 or 1.
Computers typically don't use bits individually — they group 8 bits into 1 byte.
So when a computer actually stores data, it looks like:
Which represents one character or symbol each (above being
But that's a pain to read. So by convention we split each byte into two 4-bit groups:
Bonus: 4-bit groups happen to map directly to hex digits. E.g.,
That's why we read binary as 4-bit groups by convention — easier to read, and easier to convert to hex.

What is ASCII? Why does uppercase A equal 65? This post starts with a mysterious string of numbers a senior sent from an internet café and walks through ASCII character encoding, bits, and binary conversion, with a hands-on JavaScript Console demo.
